properties.name: Site 1040 near Wadi Halfa dromedary context

popup_rows.label: Paper title
popup_rows.value: Combined hybridization capture and shotgun sequencing for ancient DNA analysis of extinct wild and domestic dromedary camel

popup_rows.label: Chronology
popup_rows.value: Historical and archaeological dromedary range context

popup_rows.label: Coordinate gazetteer
popup_rows.value: named-place resolution to Wadi Halfa city anchor

popup_rows.label: Coordinate rationale
popup_rows.value: The archived article names Site 1040 near Wadi Halfa explicitly. The current coordinate is an approximate city-level geocode for Wadi Halfa rather than a published excavation coordinate pair.

popup_rows.label: Source evidence text
popup_rows.value: The remains of a single large-sized Late Pleistocene camel individual recovered from the Site 1040 near Wadi Halfa were first evaluated by Gautier.

popup_rows.label: Nordic note
popup_rows.value: The current curated project inventory does not yet identify a shipped Nordic-localized lead for this record.

popup_rows.label: Interpretation
popup_rows.value: Camel now keeps one named-place resolution trail instead of only a broad Arabian/Levant centroid.

popup_rows.label: Warning
popup_rows.value: Coordinates are approximate, not excavation-grade exact points.

popup_rows.label: Warning
popup_rows.value: This locality is outside the Nordic lead set and remains atlas context, not Nordic-localized support.

properties.name: Botai archaeological site horse context

popup_rows.label: Paper title
popup_rows.value: Ancient genomes revisit the ancestry of domestic and Przewalski's horses

popup_rows.label: Original place text
popup_rows.value: Botai culture of the Central Asian steppes

popup_rows.label: Coordinate rationale
popup_rows.value: The archived paper names Botai explicitly, but does not ship a direct coordinate pair in the local source archive, so the current coordinate is an approximate named-site geocode.

popup_rows.label: Source evidence text
popup_rows.value: The Eneolithic Botai culture of the Central Asian steppes provides the earliest archaeological evidence for horse husbandry, ~5500 years ago, but the exact nature of early horse domestication remains controversial.

popup_rows.label: Nordic note
popup_rows.value: The current curated project inventory does not yet identify a shipped Nordic-localized lead for this record.

popup_rows.label: Interpretation
popup_rows.value: Botai remains the only clearly named domesticated-core horse site that currently earns point-level publication.

popup_rows.label: Warning
popup_rows.value: Coordinates are approximate, not excavation-grade exact points.

popup_rows.value: This locality is outside the Nordic lead set and remains atlas context, not Nordic-localized support.
